audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman phoning to ask about a summer cycling camp for her young son, who is called Charlie.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Junior Cycle Camp, Jake speaking.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Hi.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'm calling for some information about the cycle camp.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'm thinking of sending my son.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Well, it's held every weekday morning over the summer vacation.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: And we focus on basic cycling skills and safety.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: We have eight levels for children from 3 years upwards.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: How old's your son?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Charlie, he's 7.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: He can ride a bike, but he needs a little more training before he's safe to go on the road.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: He'd probably be best in level 5.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: They start off practicing on the site here, and we aim to get them riding on the road.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But first, they're taken to ride in the park, away from the traffic.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And can you tell me a bit about the instructors?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, all our staff wear different colored shirts.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: So we have three supervisors, and they have red shirts.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: They support the instructors, and they also stand in for me if I'm not around.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Then the instructors themselves are in blue shirts.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: And one of these is responsible for each class.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: In order to be accepted, all our instructors have to submit a reference from someone who's seen them work with children.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Like if they've worked as a babysitter for example.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Then they have to complete our training course, including how to do lesson plans, and generally care for the well-being of the kids in their class.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: They do a great job, I have to say.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Right, and tell me a bit about the classes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What size will Charlie's class be?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We have a limit of 8 children in each class, so their instructor really gets to know them well.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: They're out riding most of the time.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But they have quiet times too, where their instructor might tell them a story that's got something to do with cycling.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Or get them to play a game together.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: It's a lot of fun.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: It must be.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What happens if there's rain?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do the classes still run?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh yes, we don't let that put us off.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: We just put on our waterproofs.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: And keep cycling.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And is there anything special Charlie should bring along with him?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, maybe some spare clothes, especially if the weather's not so good.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: A snack for break time.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: How about a drink?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, we'll provide that.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: And make sure he has shoes, not sandals.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And just at present, Charlie has to take medication every few hours, so I'll make sure he has that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Just give us details of when he has to take it, and we'll make sure he does.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Now, there are a few things you should know about day one of the camp.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: The classes normally start at 9:30 every morning, but on day one, you should aim to get Charlie here by 9:20.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: The finishing time will be 12:30 as usual.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: We need the additional time because there are a few extra things to do.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: The most important is that we have a very careful check to make sure that every child's helmet fits properly.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: If it doesn't fit, we'll try to adjust it, or we'll find him another one.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But he must wear it all the time he's on the bike.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Then after that, all the instructors will be waiting to meet their classes, and they'll meet up in the tent.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: You can't miss it.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Each instructor will take their class away and get started.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: OK, well that all sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Now, can you tell me how much the camp costs a week?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We've managed to keep the price more or less the same as last year.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: It was 190 then.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But the places are filling up quite quickly.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, well, I'd like to book for the...
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: JUNIOR CYCLE CAMP

test_text.title: Food and Agriculture Sector Job Opportunities

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a recruitment officer called Megan Baker, giving a talk at a jobs fair about opportunities for those interested in the food and agriculture sectors.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, everyone.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: My name is Megan Baker, and I'm a recruitment consultant at AVT Recruitment Specialists.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Now, our company specializes in positions that involve working in the agriculture and horticulture sectors, so that's fresh food production.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Garden and park maintenance and so on, and these sectors do provide some very special career opportunities.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: For a start, they often offer opportunities for those who don't want to be stuck with a 40 hour week, but need to juggle work with other responsibilities, such as childcare.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: And this is very important for many of our recruits.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Some people like working in a rural setting, surrounded by plants and trees instead of buildings.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Although we can't guarantee that. But there are certainly health benefits.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Especially in jobs where you're not sitting all day looking at a screen, a big plus for many people.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Salaries can sometimes be good too, although there's a lot of variety here.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: And you may have the opportunity in some types of jobs for travel overseas, although that obviously depends on the job, and not everyone is keen to do it.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Of course, working outdoors does have its challenges.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: It's fine in summer, but can be extremely unpleasant when it's cold and windy.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: You may need to be pretty fit for some jobs, though with modern technology, that's not as important as it once was.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: And standards of health and safety are much higher now than they used to be.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: So there are fewer work related accidents, but if you like a lively city environment surrounded by lots of people, these jobs are probably not for you.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: They're often in pretty remote areas, and some people worry about finding a suitable place to live, but in our experience, this usually turns out fine.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, let me tell you about some of the exciting jobs that we have on our books right now.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: One is for a fresh Food Commercial Manager.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Our client here is a very large fresh food producer, supplying a range of top supermarkets.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: They operate in a very fast paced environment with low profit margins.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: The staff there work hard, but they play hard as well.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: So, if you have a sociable personality, this may be for you.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: We have an exciting post as an agronomist, advising farmers on issues such as crop nutrition, Protection against pests, and the latest legislation on farming and agricultural practices.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: There are good opportunities for the right person to quickly make their way up the career ladder, but a deep knowledge of the agricultural sector is expected of applicants.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: A leading supermarket is looking for a fresh produce buyer, who is available for a 12-month maternity cover contract.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: You need to have experience in administration.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Planning and buying in the fresh produce industry, and in return will receive a very competitive salary.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: We have also received a request for a Sales Manager for a chain of garden centers.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: You will be visiting centers in the region to ensure their high levels of customer service are maintained.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: This post is only suitable for someone who is prepared to live in the region.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: There is also a vacancy for a tree technician to carry out tree cutting, forestry and conservation work.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Candidates must have a clean driving license.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: And have training in safety procedures.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: A year's experience would be preferred, but the company might be prepared to consider someone who has just completed an appropriate training course.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Finally, we have a position for a farm worker.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: This will involve a wide range of farm duties, including crop sowing and harvesting.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Machine maintenance and animal care.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: Perks of the job include the possibility of renting a small cottage on the estate, and a chance to earn a competitive salary.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: A driving license and tractor driving experience are essential.
audioscript.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Candidates must have a clean driving license.
utterances.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

utterances.text: And have training in safety procedures.
utterances.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

utterances.text: A year's experience would be preferred, but the company might be prepared to consider someone who has just completed an appropriate training course.
utterances.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

utterances.text: Finally, we have a position for a farm worker.
utterances.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

utterances.text: This will involve a wide range of farm duties, including crop sowing and harvesting.
utterances.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

utterances.text: Machine maintenance and animal care.
utterances.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

utterances.text: Perks of the job include the possibility of renting a small cottage on the estate, and a chance to earn a competitive salary.
utterances.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

utterances.text: A driving license and tractor driving experience are essential.
utterances.speaker: MEGAN BAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: According to Megan, what are the TWO main advantages of working in the agriculture and horticulture sectors?
options: the active lifestyle
the above-average salaries
the flexible working opportunities
the opportunities for overseas travel
the chance to be in a natural environment

body.title: Which TWO of the following are likely to be disadvantages for people working outdoors?
options: the increasing risk of accidents
being in a very quiet location
difficult weather conditions at times
the cost of housing
the level of physical fitness required

text: What information does Megan give about each of the following job opportunities?
textReadable: What information does Megan give about each of the following job opportunities?

list: chance to earn more through overtime
items: Fresh food commercial manager <input>
Agronomist                    <input>
Fresh produce buyer           <input>
Garden centre sales manager   <input>
Tree technician               <input>
Farm worker                   <input>
body.title: Job opportunities

test_text.title: Planning a Presentation on Diet, Obesity, and Related Solutions

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two food science students called Adam and Rosie, starting to plan their presentation on diet and obesity.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, Rosie.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Shall we try to get some ideas together for our presentation on diet and obesity?
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I can talk about the experiment I did.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: To see if people can tell the difference between real sugar and artificial sweeteners.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Where you gave people drinks with either sugar or artificial sweeteners, and they had to say which they thought it was.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: It took me ages to decide exactly how I'd organize it, especially how I could make sure that people didn't know which drink I was giving them.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: It was hard to keep track of it all.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Especially as I had so many people doing it, I had to make sure I kept a proper record of what each person had had.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: So, could most people tell the difference?
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: I hadn't thought that they would be able to, but most people could.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Then there's that experiment I did, measuring the fat content of nuts to see if the nutritional information given on the packet was accurate.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: The one where you ground up the nuts and mixed them with a chemical to absorb the fat.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Yes, my results were a bit problematic.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: The fat content for that type of nut seemed much lower than it said on the package.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: But I reckon the package information was right.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I think I should probably have ground up the nuts more than I did.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: It's possible that the scales for weighing the fat weren't accurate enough too.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I'd really like to try the experiment again sometime.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So what can we say about helping people to lose weight?
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: There's a lot we could say about what restaurants could do to reduce obesity.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: I read that the items at the start of a menu and the items at the end of a menu are much more likely to be chosen than the items in the middle.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: So, if you put the low calorie items at the beginning and end of the menu, people will probably go for the food with fewer calories without even realizing what they're doing.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: I think food manufacturers could do more to encourage healthy eating.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: How?
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Well, when manufacturers put calorie counts of a food on the label,
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: they're sometimes really confusing, and I suspect they do it on purpose, because food that's high in calories tastes better, and so they'll sell more.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, so if you look at the amount of calories in a pizza.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: They'll give you the calories per quarter pizza, and you think oh that's not too bad, but who's going to eat a quarter pizza?
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I suppose another approach to this problem is to get people to exercise more.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: In England, the current guidelines are for at least 30 minutes of brisk walking.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: 5 days a week.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Now when you ask them, about 40% of men and 30% of women say they do this, but when you objectively measure the amount of walking they do with motion sensors.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: You find that only 6% of men and 4% of women do the recommended amount of exercise.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Hmm, so you can see why obesity is growing.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: So, how can people be encouraged to take more exercise?
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Well, for example, think of the location of stairs in a train station.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: If people reach the stairs before they reach the escalator when they're leaving the station, they're more likely to take the stairs, and if you increase the width of the stairs.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: You'll get more people using them at the same time.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: It's an unconscious process and influenced by minor modifications in their environment.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: And it might not be a big change, but if it happens every day, it all adds up.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Yes, but actually I'm not sure if we should be talking about exercise in our presentation.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Well, we've done quite a bit of reading about it.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: I know, but it's going to mean we have a very wide focus, and our tutor did say that we need to focus on causes and solutions in terms of nutrition.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Oh, I suppose so.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: And we've got plenty of information about that.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: OK, well, that will be simpler.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: So what shall we do now?
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: We've still got half an hour before our next lecture.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: Let's think about what we're going to include, and what will go where.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: Then we can decide what slides we need.
audioscript.speaker: ROSIE

audioscript.text: OK, fine.
audioscript.speaker: ADAM

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO points does Adam make about his experiment on artificial sweeteners?
options: The results were what he had predicted.
The experiment was simple to set up.
A large sample of people was tested.
The subjects were unaware of what they were drinking.
The test was repeated several times for each person.

body.title: Which TWO problems did Rosie have when measuring the fat content of nuts?
options: She used the wrong sort of nuts.
She used an unsuitable chemical.
She did not grind the nuts finely enough.
The information on the nut package was incorrect.
The weighing scales may have been unsuitable.

items.title: Adam suggests that restaurants could reduce obesity if their menus
options: offered fewer options.
had more low-calorie foods.
were organised in a particular way.

items.title: The students agree that food manufacturers deliberately
options: make calorie counts hard to understand.
fail to provide accurate calorie counts.
use ineffective methods to reduce calories.

items.title: What does Rosie say about levels of exercise in England?
options: The amount recommended is much too low.
Most people overestimate how much they do.
Women now exercise more than they used to.

items.title: Adam refers to the location and width of stairs in a train station to illustrate
options: practical changes that can influence people's behaviour.
methods of helping people who have mobility problems.
ways of preventing accidents by controlling crowd movement.

items.title: What do the students agree about including reference to exercise in their presentation?
options: They should probably leave it out.
They need to do more research on it.
They should discuss this with their tutor.

items.title: What are the students going to do next for their presentation?
options: prepare some slides for it
find out how long they have for it
decide on its content and organisation

test_text.title: The Revival and Significance of Hand Knitting

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear a lecturer on a fashion design course introducing the subject of hand knitting, which involves making things like woolen jumpers and scarves.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone, so today we're going to look at an important creative activity, and that's hand knitting.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ancient knitted garments have been found in many different countries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Showing that knitting is a global activity with a long history.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When someone says the word knitting, we might well picture an elderly person.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A grandmother perhaps, sitting by the fire, knitting garments for herself or other members of the family.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's a homely image.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But one that may lead you to feel that knitting is an activity of the past, and indeed during the previous decade, it was one of the skills that was predicted to vanish from everyday life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For although humans have sewn and knitted their own clothing for a very long time, many of these craft-based skills went into decline.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When industrial machines took over, mainly because they were no longer passed down from one generation to another.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, that's all changing, and interest in knitting classes in many countries is actually rising.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As more and more people are seeking formal instruction in the skill. With that trend, we're also seeing an increase in the sales figures,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: for knitting equipment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, why do people want to be taught to knit at a time when a machine can readily do the job for them?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The answer is that knitting as a handicraft has numerous benefits for those doing it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's consider what some of these might be.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: While many people knitted garments in the past, because they couldn't afford to buy clothes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's still true today that knitting can be helpful, if you're experiencing economic hardship.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you have several children who all need warm winter clothes, knitting may save you a lot of money. And the results of knitting your own clothes, can be very rewarding,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: even though the skills you need to get going are really quite basic, and the financial outlay is minimal.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the more significant benefits in today's world are to do with well-being.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In a world where it's estimated that we spend up to nine hours a day online.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Doing something with our hands that is craft based makes us feel good.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It releases us from the stress of a technological fast paced life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, let's look back a bit to early knitting activities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, no one really knows when knitting first began.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But archaeological remains have disclosed plenty of information for us to think about.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the interesting things about knitting is that the earliest pieces of clothing that have been found suggest that most of the items produced were round rather than flat.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Discoveries from the 3rd and 4th centuries in Egypt show that things like socks and gloves that were needed to keep hands and feet warm were knitted in one piece using four or five needles.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's very different.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: From most knitting patterns today, which only require two.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What's more, the very first needles people used were hand carved out of wood.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And other natural materials like bone, whereas today's needles are largely made of steel or plastic, and make that characteristic clicking sound.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When someone's using them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ancient people knitted using yarns made from linen, hemp, cotton, and wool.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And these were often very rough on the skin.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The spinning wheel, which allowed people to make finer yarns, and produce much greater quantities of them, led to the dominance of wool in the knitting industry.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Wool was often favored for its warmth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another interesting fact about knitting is that because it was practiced in so many parts of the world, for so many purposes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Regional differences in style developed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This visual identity has allowed researchers to match bits of knitted clothing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That have been unearthed over time to the region from which the wearer came, or the job that he or she did.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As I've mentioned, knitting offered people from poor communities a way of making extra money while doing other tasks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For many centuries, it seems, men, women and children took every opportunity to knit, for example, while watching over sheep, walking to market.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or riding in boats.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So let's move on to take...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Doing something with our hands that is craft based makes us feel good.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It releases us from the stress of a technological fast paced life.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, let's look back a bit to early knitting activities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, no one really knows when knitting first began.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But archaeological remains have disclosed plenty of information for us to think about.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of the interesting things about knitting is that the earliest pieces of clothing that have been found suggest that most of the items produced were round rather than flat.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Discoveries from the 3rd and 4th centuries in Egypt show that things like socks and gloves that were needed to keep hands and feet warm were knitted in one piece using four or five needles.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's very different.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: From most knitting patterns today, which only require two.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What's more, the very first needles people used were hand carved out of wood.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And other natural materials like bone, whereas today's needles are largely made of steel or plastic, and make that characteristic clicking sound.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When someone's using them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Ancient people knitted using yarns made from linen, hemp, cotton, and wool.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And these were often very rough on the skin.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The spinning wheel, which allowed people to make finer yarns, and produce much greater quantities of them, led to the dominance of wool in the knitting industry.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Wool was often favored for its warmth.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another interesting fact about knitting is that because it was practiced in so many parts of the world, for so many purposes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Regional differences in style developed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This visual identity has allowed researchers to match bits of knitted clothing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That have been unearthed over time to the region from which the wearer came, or the job that he or she did.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As I've mentioned, knitting offered people from poor communities a way of making extra money while doing other tasks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For many centuries, it seems, men, women and children took every opportunity to knit, for example, while watching over sheep, walking to market.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or riding in boats.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So let's move on to take...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
